event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers begin settling the region, leaving stone tools and the earliest evidence of human presence in present-day Uruguay.
Early peoples build the cerritos de indios, large earthen mounds in the eastern wetlands used for habitation, burial, and ceremony.
The Charrúa, Chaná, Guaraní, and other indigenous groups inhabit the territory, living as hunter-gatherers and fishers along rivers and the coast.
Spanish explorer Juan Díaz de Solís lands near the Río de la Plata and is killed by indigenous people, marking the first recorded European contact.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition explores the Río de la Plata estuary while searching for a passage to the Pacific.
Governor Hernando Arias de Saavedra (Hernandarias) introduces cattle and horses, which multiply on the grasslands and shape the future ranching economy.
Spanish missionaries establish a settlement at Santo Domingo Soriano, among the earliest European foundations in Uruguay.
Portugal founds Colonia del Sacramento on the Río de la Plata, igniting a long Spanish-Portuguese contest over the territory.
Bruno Mauricio de Zabala founds Montevideo as a fortified Spanish city to counter Portuguese expansion.
The region becomes part of the newly created Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata, with administration centered in Buenos Aires.
British forces invade the Río de la Plata during the British invasions, capturing Montevideo in early 1807 before withdrawing.
José Gervasio Artigas leads the revolt against Spanish rule, winning the Battle of Las Piedras and emerging as the father of Uruguayan independence.
Artigas issues the Instructions of the Year XIII, calling for federalism and autonomy within a confederation of the Río de la Plata provinces.
Artigas establishes a federal league and enacts a pioneering agrarian reform redistributing land to the poor and indigenous people.
Portuguese-Brazilian forces invade the Banda Oriental, beginning an occupation that would eventually defeat Artigas.
Artigas is defeated and goes into permanent exile in Paraguay as Portuguese forces consolidate control.
The territory is annexed to the Portuguese (later Brazilian) empire as the Cisplatine Province.
The Thirty-Three Orientals led by Juan Antonio Lavalleja launch a liberation campaign and declare independence from Brazil.
The Treaty of Montevideo, brokered by Britain, ends the Cisplatine War and creates Uruguay as an independent buffer state.
Uruguay adopts its first constitution, formally establishing the República Oriental del Uruguay.
Government forces massacre much of the remaining Charrúa population at Salsipuedes, devastating the indigenous people.
The Guerra Grande begins, a prolonged civil war between the Colorado and Blanco parties entangled with regional powers.
The Great Siege of Montevideo begins, lasting nearly nine years amid the Guerra Grande.
The Guerra Grande ends, leaving the Colorado and Blanco rivalry entrenched in Uruguayan politics.
Uruguay joins Argentina and Brazil in the Triple Alliance against Paraguay in the devastating War of the Triple Alliance.
Colonel Lorenzo Latorre seizes power, beginning a period of militarist rule that modernizes administration and the economy.
José Batlle y Ordóñez becomes president and launches sweeping social, political, and economic reforms shaping modern Uruguay.
Government forces defeat the Blanco revolt led by Aparicio Saravia, ending major armed conflict between the traditional parties.
A new constitution institutionalizes Batlle's reforms, separating church and state and creating a plural executive council.
Uruguay hosts and wins the first FIFA World Cup, defeating Argentina in the final in Montevideo.
President Gabriel Terra carries out a self-coup, dissolving parliament and establishing an authoritarian regime.
Constitutional democracy is restored after the Terra-era regime, and Uruguay later aligns with the Allies in World War II.
Uruguay becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Uruguay wins its second FIFA World Cup in the 'Maracanazo,' stunning host Brazil in Rio de Janeiro's final match.
A constitutional reform replaces the presidency with a nine-member collegiate executive council.
The Blanco (National) Party wins national elections, ending more than nine decades of Colorado dominance.
A new constitution restores the single presidency, and the urban guerrilla movement Tupamaros intensifies its activities.
President Juan María Bordaberry, backed by the military, dissolves parliament and ushers in a civic-military dictatorship.
The military removes Bordaberry and rules directly, presiding over widespread repression, torture, and disappearances.
Voters reject a military-drafted constitution in a plebiscite, signaling popular resistance to continued authoritarian rule.
Democracy is restored as Julio María Sanguinetti takes office as the first elected president after the dictatorship.
A referendum upholds the amnesty law shielding military officers from prosecution for human rights abuses.
Uruguay co-founds Mercosur, the South American trade bloc, signing the Treaty of Asunción with Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay.
A severe banking and economic crisis, spilling over from Argentina, triggers a deep recession and financial turmoil.
The leftist Frente Amplio coalition wins the presidency under Tabaré Vázquez, breaking the two-party monopoly on power.
Former Tupamaro guerrilla José 'Pepe' Mujica becomes president, gaining global renown for his austere, humble lifestyle.
Uruguay legalizes same-sex marriage and becomes the first country in the world to fully legalize and regulate the cannabis market.
Luis Lacalle Pou of the National Party takes office, returning the center-right to power after fifteen years of Frente Amplio rule.
Frente Amplio's Yamandú Orsi assumes the presidency after winning the 2024 runoff, restoring the left to power.
